name: Constructions — Compass and Straightedge
whatIsIt: Constructions are exact drawings made with only a compass and a straightedge (ruler with no measuring). Core moves: bisect a line segment (build its perpendicular bisector), bisect an angle, copy an angle, make a 60° angle, and divide a segment into a given ratio by marking equal arcs on a ray. No protractor — the compass does the measuring.
whyNeeded: Constructions teach WHY geometry works, not just answers. They are the basis of drafting, engineering drawings, tiling and design, where you must reproduce exact angles and equal divisions without a scale. They also prove that equal lengths and equal angles can be created purely from circles.
realLifeUses: Marking the exact middle of a plank without measuring (perpendicular bisector)
Splitting a strip of land into a fixed ratio of parts
Drafting precise 60° and 30° angles in design
Copying an angle from one drawing to another

vocabulary.meaning: A line that cuts a segment into two equal halves at 90°; every point on it is equidistant from the two ends.
A ray that splits an angle into two equal angles.
A ruler used only to draw straight lines — its markings are not used to measure.
Part of a circle drawn by the compass; where arcs cross gives exact points.

story.title: The Fair Rope Cut
story.text: Zoya must cut a 8 cm strip exactly in half, but her ruler's numbers have rubbed off. Master Ji shows her a construction: open the compass wide, draw arcs from each end above and below, join where they cross. That line is the perpendicular bisector — it meets the strip at its exact midpoint, 4 cm from each end. 'The compass measured it for you,' he smiles.
story.extractedProblem: An 8 cm segment is cut by its perpendicular bisector. How far is the midpoint from each end?
story.answerInStory: 4 cm — the perpendicular bisector splits 8 cm into two equal halves of 4 cm.

shapes.label: perpendicular bisector: arcs from each end cross above and below

visual.caption: Arcs of equal radius from both ends cross at two points; the line through them is the perpendicular bisector, meeting the segment at its exact midpoint.

teachingGallery.title: Arcs create exact points
teachingGallery.note: Equal-radius arcs from two centres cross at points that are exactly equidistant — the key to every construction.

shapes.label: angle bisector splits an angle in two

examples.caption: One arc across the angle, then two equal arcs, give the bisector.
A compass arc equal to the base makes a perfect 60° angle.
To divide a segment in ratio 2:3, step off 5 equal arcs on a ray.

standardMethod.summary: Use equal compass arcs to locate exact points, then join with the straightedge. Perpendicular bisector: arcs from both ends. Angle bisector: arc across the angle, then two equal arcs. Ratio division: mark (sum of ratio) equal arcs on a ray.
steps: For a perpendicular bisector: from each end, draw arcs of the SAME radius (more than half the length) above and below; join the two crossings.
For an angle bisector: draw an arc cutting both arms, then equal arcs from those two points; join the vertex to where they cross.
For a 60° angle: from the vertex draw an arc; keep the same radius and mark it once along the arc; join — that is 60°.
To divide a segment in ratio m:n, draw a ray, step off (m + n) equal arcs, and connect the last to the endpoint, then draw parallels.
Check with the definition: halves equal, or the two new angles equal.

workedExamples.problem: A 10 cm segment gets a perpendicular bisector. Where does it meet the segment?
steps: The perpendicular bisector passes through the midpoint.

workedExamples.problem: A 90° angle is bisected. What is each new angle?
steps: Bisecting splits the angle into two equal parts.

commonMistakes.mistake: Changing the compass opening between the two arcs of a bisector.
commonMistakes.fix: The arcs must have the SAME radius. Keep the compass fixed so the crossing points are truly equidistant.

commonMistakes.mistake: Marking m or n arcs instead of (m + n) when dividing a segment in ratio m:n.
commonMistakes.fix: Step off the SUM of the ratio parts. For 2:3 that is 2 + 3 = 5 equal arcs, then split at the 2nd mark.

easy.q: A perpendicular bisector meets a 6 cm segment at its midpoint. How far is that from each end?
Bisecting a 60° angle gives two angles of how many degrees each?

medium.q: To divide a segment in the ratio 2:3, how many equal arcs do you step off on the ray?
A perpendicular bisector meets the segment at what angle (degrees)?

challenge.q: To divide a segment in the ratio 3:4, how many equal arcs on the ray?
Amir changes his compass width between the two bisector arcs. What's the fix?

options.label: Keep the SAME radius for both arcs

challenge.answer: Keep the SAME radius for both arcs

questions.q: Perpendicular bisector of a 12 cm segment — distance from midpoint to each end?
Bisecting a 90° angle gives each angle as?
Dividing a segment in ratio 5:1 needs how many equal arcs?
In a pure construction you may use…

options.label: Only a compass and a straightedge (no protractor)

questions.answer: Only a compass and a straightedge (no protractor)

teachBackPrompt: With a compass, show a grown-up how equal arcs from both ends of a line cross to give its exact midpoint (the perpendicular bisector), and explain why the two halves must be equal and meet at 90°.

revisionCard.summary: Constructions use only compass + straightedge. Perpendicular bisector: equal arcs from both ends → midpoint at 90°. Angle bisector: arc across arms then equal arcs → splits the angle in two. 60° angle: one radius stepped along an arc. Ratio m:n: step off (m + n) equal arcs. Never change the radius mid-arc.
